channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Prosecutors Make Surprise Visit to Fed as Pirro Defends Investigation
item.description: The Justice Department’s criminal inquiry into the Federal Reserve threatens to delay the confirmation of the next chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: An overview showing construction at the Federal Reserve last year. The $2.5 billion renovation project is at the center of an investigation into the central bank.

item.title: JD Vance Heckled In Antiwar Protest at Turning Point USA Event
item.description: Vice President JD Vance appeared to express sympathy with critics of the war with Iran: “I recognize that young voters do not love the policy we have in the Middle East, OK. I understand.”
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Chip Somodevilla
item.media:description: Mr. Vance, right, addressed the audience outbursts Tuesday, agreeing that “Jesus Christ does not support genocide” and defending the Trump administration’s Middle East policies

item.title: Vance Says Pope Leo Should Be More Careful When Talking About Theology
item.description: The vice president, who is Catholic, took issue with Pope Leo XIV’s statement that disciples of Christ are “never on the side of those who once wielded the sword and today drop bombs.”
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Alyssa Pointer/Reuters
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance spoke at a Turning Point USA event in Athens, Ga., on Tuesday.

item.title: Rubio Hosts Israel and Lebanon for Rare Meeting Shadowed by U.S.-Iran War
item.description: The gathering ended with encouraging words, even as Israel continued to refuse to halt its military campaign against Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Oliver Contreras/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio meeting with officials from Lebanon and Israel at the State Department in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: House Passes Air Safety Bill, Setting Up Clash with Senate
item.description: Senate leaders say key provisions fall short of what is necessary to prevent aircraft from midair collisions.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: Swalwell’s Exit Injects ‘Chaos’ Into California Governor’s Race
item.description: Democratic candidates see sudden voter interest in the sleepy contest as a campaign opportunity. All are scrambling for support from former backers of Eric Swalwell.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Eric Swalwell’s departure seemed most likely to benefit the two Democrats who had been polling closest to him.

item.title: Captains of Boat That Capsized Near San Diego Plead Guilty to 4 Deaths
item.description: The boat was carrying 19 migrants from Mexico to Southern California when it overturned in May 2025, the authorities said. Two of the victims were children.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Denis Poroy/Associated Press
item.media:description: The capsized boat last year at Torrey Pines State Beach in San Diego.

item.title: Trump’s Blockade Risks Upending an Emerging Détente With China
item.description: In a thinly veiled critique of the war in Iran, China’s leader said the world could not risk reverting “to the law of the jungle.”
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with China’s leader, Xi Jinping, in South Korea last October. They will meet again next month.

item.title: Pentagon Says It Attacked Another Boat in the Pacific, Killing 4
item.description: It was the second strike in two days, in a campaign against those the United States accuses of drug smuggling.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Southern Command, led by Gen. Francis L. Donovan of the Marine Corps, announced the latest strike on Tuesday.

item.title: Gallego Dismissed Rumors of a ‘Flirty’ Swalwell, Highlighting a Culture of Silence
item.description: The admission by Senator Ruben Gallego that he had heard, but disbelieved, rumors about Eric Swalwell and women showed the attitude on Capitol Hill toward men accused of behaving badly.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Ruben Gallego at the Capitol on Tuesday. He spent considerable time with Mr. Swalwell and chaired his short-lived 2020 presidential campaign.

item.title: Surgeon Who Removed Wrong Organ From Patient Is Charged in His Death
item.description: Dr. Thomas Shaknovsky tried to persuade his colleagues in the operating room that the liver he removed from a 70-year-old patient was a spleen, according to Florida’s Health Department.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: via the Bryan family
item.media:description: William and Beverly Bryan.

item.title: Justice Dept. Moves to Vacate Jan. 6 Convictions for Far-Right Extremists
item.description: Defending the convictions would likely have required administration officials to assert that far-right groups were acting on behalf of President Trump on Jan. 6, 2021.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump released Stewart Rhodes, the founder and leader of the Oath Keepers, from prison but did not grant him a full pardon.

item.title: Former U.C.L.A. Gynecologist Is Sentenced to 11 Years for Sexual Abuse
item.description: James M. Heaps pleaded guilty on Tuesday to sexually abusing patients, sparing them from having to testify at another trial after his conviction was overturned in February.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Al Seib/Los Angeles Times, via Getty Images
item.media:description: James M. Heaps in Los Angeles County Superior Court in 2020. He pleaded guilty to 13 felony sex abuse counts on Tuesday.

item.title: Hampshire College Will Close Amid Student Enrollment Declines
item.description: Other small private colleges like Hampshire have closed in recent years as financial pressures and competition for students increase.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: David L. Ryan/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hampshire College’s campus in Amherst, Mass.

item.title: Resignations of Eric Swalwell and Tony Gonzales Set Up Special Election Fights in Texas and California
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California called a special election to replace Mr. Swalwell, while Gov. Greg Abbott of Texas had yet to announce one for Mr. Gonzales’s successor.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Laurel Rosenhall

item.title: 51 Percent of Americans Think Trump’s Military Action in Iran Has Not Been Worthwhile
item.description: A survey from Ipsos and Reuters, released on Tuesday, found few Americans — 24 percent — think the war in Iran has been worth the costs and benefits.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael Ciaglo for The New York Times

item.title: A New Accuser Says Eric Swalwell Sexually Assaulted Her
item.description: The woman said Mr. Swalwell, who resigned from Congress on Tuesday afternoon, raped her in a West Hollywood hotel room in 2018. She said she believed she was drugged.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan and Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lonna Drewes, left, said she had waited years to speak, because of “fear, not doubt.”

item.title: Camp Mystic Hearing in Texas Weighs Reopening After Deadly Flooding
item.description: Testimony at a hearing this week has focused on what camp leaders knew and did as floodwaters rose in July, killing at least 116 people.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Generations of girls have spent their summers at Camp Mystic, nestled along the Guadalupe River in the Texas Hill Country.

item.title: Ishmael Jaffree, Who Won Case Rejecting School Prayer, Is Dead at 80
item.description: An Alabama parent, he objected to prayer in his children’s classrooms. The Supreme Court ruled for him, a high-water mark in the push for the strict separation of church and state.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Bettmann Archive/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ishmael Jaffree outside the U.S. Supreme Court in 1984. The next year, the court ruled in his favor in his challenge to Alabama laws that had made it easier to bring prayer into the classroom.

item.title: Appeals Court Ends Contempt Inquiry Into Trump Administration’s Deportation Flights
item.description: A federal judge’s nearly yearlong effort to investigate whether the Trump administration had violated his order had become a point of contention in the president’s battles with the courts.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Judge James E. Boasberg, the chief federal judge in Washington, has been a focus of the Trump administration’s ire as the president and his aides have repeatedly assailed him for his decisions in the deportation case.

item.title: Maryland Governor and State Democrats Fail in Redistricting Effort
item.description: State Democrats butted heads over a gerrymandering plan that could have eliminated the state’s lone Republican seat in the U.S. House.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin
item.media:credit: Bryan Woolston/Associated Press
item.media:description: Bill Ferguson, left, Maryland’s Senate president, had been critical of a redistricting attempt championed by Gov. Wes Moore.

item.title: Trump Administration Accuses Biden DOJ of Unfairly Prosecuting Anti-Abortion Activists
item.description: The report is part of the president’s effort to claim anti-conservative and anti-Christian biases in federal law enforcement, even as he pushes to wield the legal system against his political enemies.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department’s efforts to end what it calls “weaponization” of law enforcement have moved on multiple tracks.

item.title: Texas A&M Picks an Insider for President After Months of Conflict
item.description: Debates over how to teach about gender, sexuality and other topics have shaken the school, and lead to the ouster of the previous president at the College Station campus last summer.
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel
item.media:credit: Annie Mulligan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Texas A&M campus in College Station.

item.title: House Republicans Step Up Scrutiny of Democratic Fund-Raising Giant
item.description: Three Republican-led committees, responding to a New York Times report this month, accused ActBlue of withholding documents from a subpoena request.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Shane Goldmacher
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/Reuters
item.media:description: Regina Wallace-Jones, the ActBlue chief executive, at the 2024 Democratic National Convention in Chicago.

item.title: Virginia Governor Ends Tax Breaks for Confederate Groups
item.description: The new law signed by Gov. Abigail Spanberger is the culmination of a long, Democrat-led push to distance Virginia from its Confederate past.
item.dc:creator: Anna Venarchik
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Abigail Spanberger of Virginia speaking in Williamsburg, Va., in February.

item.title: Olivia Troye, Ex-Pence Aide, Runs for the House as a Democrat
item.description: After spending most of her Washington career as a Republican, she joins a crowded field of Democrats running in a Virginia district that doesn’t exist yet.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Leah Millis/Reuters
item.media:description: Olivia Troye served as a national security aide to former Vice President Mike Pence until the summer of 2020.

item.title: Los Angeles Schools Strike Narrowly Averted in Last-Minute Deal
item.description: The strike would have shut down classes on Tuesday for hundreds of thousands of students in the nation’s second-largest school district.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler and Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Gabriella Angotti-Jones for The New York Times
item.media:description: Students in Los Angeles walk to class in a photo from 2025.

item.title: In Leo, Trump Faces a Different Kind of Papal Opponent
item.description: Unlike his predecessor, Pope Leo XIV enjoys growing support from a broad swath of conservative Catholics.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Dias and Motoko Rich
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Vatican, Pope Leo presents a new foil for President Trump.

item.title: Trump’s Attacks on Pope Leo Create Fresh Midterm Headaches for G.O.P.
item.description: Republicans are counting on the votes of Catholics to maintain control of Congress.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has a long history of publicly clashing with the pope. He spent years tangling with Pope Francis over his immigration policies.

item.title: How Stephen Miller Is Adjusting Trump’s Immigration Agenda
item.description: After the chaos and death that ensued during the deportation raids in Minneapolis, Stephen Miller, the architect of President Trump’s mass deportation campaign, is changing course on immigration. Our White House correspondent Zolan Kanno-Youngs explains how the administration’s strategy is shifting.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Gilad Thaler, Jon Miller, Nikolay Nikolov, Stephanie Swart, June Kim, Rafaela Balster, Paul Abowd and Pierre Kattar

item.title: At a House Republican’s New York Event, Flashes of an Iran Backlash
item.description: Representative Mike Lawler, who is seeking re-election in a swing district in the Hudson Valley, faced tough questions from constituents about his stance on the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:credit: Gregg Vigliotti for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Mike Lawler listening onstage to an attendee at a town hall event last weekend.

item.title: Undocumented Immigrants Fear Tax Data Will Be Shared With ICE
item.description: Fears that the I.R.S. could share their data with ICE have turned tax season into a gamble for people who are in the country illegally.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Many undocumented immigrants who want to file tax returns have sought the help of clinics like this one in Los Angeles.

item.title: A Divided America Processes a War With Iran
item.description: As the war in Iran extends into its seventh week and a truce feels increasingly shaky, many Americans expressed bewilderment about a conflict that came with little warning.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy, Pooja Salhotra, Jazmine Ulloa, Anna Griffin, Emily Cataneo and Ruth Igielnik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
